frameworks.name: Bayesian observer model
frameworks.one_line: optimal perceptual inference combining prior with likelihood explains motion estimation biases

frameworks.name: artificial neural network (deep classifier)
frameworks.one_line: ANN trained to categorize translational motion of natural images, reinterpreted in Bayesian terms

frameworks.name: slow-speed prior
frameworks.one_line: argued NOT to arise from a training-distribution prior but from feature-space geometry

core_question: Can perceptual biases of a motion-categorization ANN be understood as emerging Bayesian computations?
key_assumptions: ANNs and Bayesian observers share the premise that vision approximates optimal behavior
perceptual biases reflect an effective prior implicit in the model
training-sample distribution alone does not determine the embedded prior
manipulations: feature-space geometry of motion categories in 2D velocity space
categorical loss penalty across the speed axis
network architecture varied to assess effect on sensory uncertainty
key_findings: ANN trained on uniform motion categories showed human-like slow-speed bias
category spacing in 2D velocity space was non-uniform despite uniform sample counts
categorical loss penalized low-speed errors more strongly due to closer category spacing
accounting for feature-space geometry aligned ANN speed estimates with a Bayesian observer
interpretations: feature-space geometry and loss shape together embed an effective slow-speed prior in the ANN
sensory uncertainty in the ANN depends on network architecture, paralleling likelihood width in Bayesian models
